DLF.revenueModel: Earns from selling residential developments (lumpy, project-linked) and from recurring rental income on its commercial office and retail portfolio. The annuity rental business adds stability to development cash flows.

LODHA.coreBusiness: A large residential real-estate developer (Macrotech Developers, Lodha brand) focused on the Mumbai region and Pune, across affordable, mid-market and premium housing, with a growing logistics/industrial-parks arm.
LODHA.revenueModel: Earns mainly from selling residential developments (project-linked), with a growing annuity stream from logistics and commercial assets. Pre-sales (bookings), execution pace and the housing cycle drive revenue.

GODREJPROP.coreBusiness: A leading residential real-estate developer (Godrej Properties) operating across major Indian cities, often through joint-development and asset-light partnerships, leveraging the Godrej brand.
GODREJPROP.revenueModel: Earns from selling residential developments (project-linked); a capital-efficient, joint-development model drives expansion. Pre-sales, project launches and the housing cycle drive revenue.

OBEROIRLTY.coreBusiness: A premium Mumbai-focused real-estate developer (Oberoi Realty) building high-end residential projects and owning commercial offices, malls and a hotel — a developer plus an annuity-asset owner.
OBEROIRLTY.revenueModel: Earns from selling premium residential developments and from recurring rental income on its commercial and retail assets (plus hospitality). The annuity portfolio balances lumpy residential cash flows.

PRESTIGE.coreBusiness: A diversified real-estate developer (Prestige Estates) strong in south India (especially Bengaluru), spanning residential, commercial offices, retail and hospitality, with a growing annuity portfolio.
PRESTIGE.revenueModel: Earns from selling residential developments (project-linked) and from recurring rental income on commercial, retail and hospitality assets. Pre-sales and the build-out of annuity assets drive revenue.

PHOENIXLTD.coreBusiness: India's largest retail-mall developer and operator (Phoenix Mills), owning and running flagship shopping malls, with associated commercial offices, hospitality and some residential.
PHOENIXLTD.revenueModel: Earns recurring rental income (often linked to tenant sales) from its malls — a stable, consumption-linked annuity model — plus offices, hospitality and residential. Mall footfalls and consumption drive revenue.

BRIGADE.coreBusiness: A south-India-focused real-estate developer (Brigade Enterprises), strong in Bengaluru, across residential, commercial offices, retail and hospitality.
BRIGADE.revenueModel: Earns from selling residential developments (project-linked) and recurring rental income from commercial, retail and hospitality assets. Pre-sales and annuity-asset growth drive revenue.

ADANIPORTS.coreBusiness: India's largest private port operator (Adani Ports & SEZ), owning and operating a network of ports and terminals along India's coast, plus logistics, rail and SEZ businesses.
ADANIPORTS.revenueModel: Earns from cargo-handling charges at its ports and from logistics services — a volume-and-tariff-driven model. Cargo throughput, port mix and integrated logistics drive revenue; trade volumes are the key variable.

GMRAIRPORT.coreBusiness: An airport developer and operator (GMR Airports) running major Indian airports including Delhi and Hyderabad, plus overseas airport interests — earning from both aeronautical and commercial (retail, real estate) airport activities.
GMRAIRPORT.revenueModel: Earns regulated aeronautical charges (per passenger/aircraft) and higher-margin non-aeronautical income (retail, F&B, advertising, land monetisation) at its airports. Passenger traffic is the core driver.

CONCOR.coreBusiness: A government-owned container logistics company (Container Corporation of India) operating rail-based container transport and a network of inland container depots and terminals across the country.
CONCOR.revenueModel: Earns from rail freight on containers and from terminal/handling and warehousing services — a volume-and-tariff model tied to EXIM and domestic trade. Container volumes and the rail-versus-road mix drive revenue.

BLUEDART.coreBusiness: India's leading premium express-logistics and parcel-delivery company (Blue Dart, part of DHL), offering time-definite air and ground express delivery for documents and parcels, including e-commerce.
BLUEDART.revenueModel: Earns per-shipment charges on express deliveries, with a premium positioning. Shipment volumes (including e-commerce), yields and its dedicated air network drive revenue.

DELHIVERY.coreBusiness: A technology-led third-party logistics company (Delhivery) providing parcel express, partial-truckload freight, warehousing and supply-chain services — a major logistics backbone for Indian e-commerce.
DELHIVERY.revenueModel: Earns from shipping and supply-chain services charged to e-commerce and enterprise clients. Volume scale, network density and a technology-driven cost structure drive revenue and the path to profitability.

INDIGO.coreBusiness: India's largest airline (IndiGo, operated by InterGlobe Aviation), a low-cost carrier with a dominant domestic market share and a fast-growing international network, operating a large, mostly single-type fleet.
INDIGO.revenueModel: Earns from passenger ticket sales plus ancillary revenue (baggage, seats, food) and cargo. A low-cost model — high aircraft utilisation, single fleet type, lean costs — drives its competitive position; fuel and currency are key variables.

INDHOTEL.coreBusiness: India's largest hospitality company (Indian Hotels Company, Tata Group), operating hotels and resorts under the Taj, SeleQtions, Vivanta and Ginger brands, plus newer businesses like Qmin and Ama Stays.
INDHOTEL.revenueModel: Earns from room revenue, food and beverage, and management fees on operated hotels. Occupancy, average room rates (ARR) and an asset-light management-contract push drive revenue; tourism cycles matter.

IRCTC.coreBusiness: A government-owned company (Indian Railway Catering and Tourism Corporation) with a near-monopoly on online railway-ticket booking, plus railway catering, packaged drinking water (Rail Neer) and tourism services.
IRCTC.revenueModel: Earns convenience fees on online train-ticket bookings (high-margin, near-monopoly), plus catering, Rail Neer water sales and tourism packages. Ticketing volumes and the convenience fee drive profitability.

RVNL.coreBusiness: A government-owned rail-infrastructure company (Rail Vikas Nigam) that executes railway projects — new lines, electrification, doubling, bridges and related works — largely on behalf of Indian Railways.
RVNL.revenueModel: Earns by executing railway-infrastructure projects (project-linked, recognised over time), historically on a cost-plus/margin basis for the Railways, now also bidding competitively. The railway capex cycle drives its order book and revenue.

HAL.coreBusiness: India's largest defence-aerospace company (Hindustan Aeronautics), government-owned, designing, building and maintaining military aircraft, helicopters, engines and avionics, mainly for the Indian armed forces.
HAL.revenueModel: Earns from manufacturing aircraft/helicopters and from a large, recurring repair-overhaul-and-spares (services) business for the defence forces. A big order book and indigenisation drive long-term revenue.

BEL.coreBusiness: A government-owned defence-electronics company (Bharat Electronics) making radars, communication, electronic-warfare, missile-electronics and other systems mainly for the Indian armed forces, plus some non-defence electronics.
BEL.revenueModel: Earns from manufacturing defence-electronics systems under government orders (project-linked) plus services and spares. A large defence order book and the indigenisation push drive revenue.

BDL.coreBusiness: A government-owned defence company (Bharat Dynamics) that manufactures missiles, guided weapons, torpedoes and related defence systems, primarily for the Indian armed forces.
BDL.revenueModel: Earns from manufacturing missiles and guided-weapon systems under defence-ministry orders (project-linked), plus services. The defence order book, indigenisation and export potential drive revenue.

MAZDOCK.coreBusiness: A government-owned defence shipyard (Mazagon Dock Shipbuilders) that builds warships and submarines for the Indian Navy — one of India's premier naval shipbuilders, notably for submarines and destroyers.
MAZDOCK.revenueModel: Earns by executing long-duration warship and submarine construction contracts for the Navy, recognised over multi-year build periods. A large defence order book gives revenue visibility.

COCHINSHIP.coreBusiness: A government-owned shipyard (Cochin Shipyard) that builds and repairs ships — including defence vessels (notably India's indigenous aircraft carrier) and commercial ships — with a large ship-repair business.
COCHINSHIP.revenueModel: Earns from shipbuilding contracts (defence and commercial, project-linked over multi-year periods) and a recurring ship-repair business. The defence order book and repair demand drive revenue.

DATAPATTNS.coreBusiness: A defence-and-aerospace electronics company (Data Patterns) that designs and makes electronic systems and sub-systems — radars, avionics, electronic warfare and test systems — with strong in-house design capability.
DATAPATTNS.revenueModel: Earns from designing and manufacturing defence-electronics systems under government and OEM orders (project-linked), plus services. Vertical integration and in-house IP support high margins; the defence order book drives growth.

HAVELLS.coreBusiness: A leading electrical and consumer-durables company (Havells) making cables and wires, switchgear, lighting, fans and a range of consumer appliances (including the Lloyd brand in ACs and white goods).
HAVELLS.revenueModel: Earns from selling electrical products and consumer durables through a wide branded distribution network. Brand strength, premiumisation and category expansion (e.g. Lloyd) drive revenue.

POLYCAB.coreBusiness: India's largest wires-and-cables maker (Polycab), supplying electrical wires and cables for construction, infrastructure and industry, plus a growing fast-moving electrical goods (fans, lighting, switches) business.
POLYCAB.revenueModel: Earns mainly from selling wires and cables to construction, infrastructure and industrial buyers, plus branded consumer electrical goods. Volumes, copper/aluminium costs and the FMEG growth push drive revenue.

KEI.coreBusiness: A wires-and-cables maker (KEI Industries) producing house wires, power and specialty cables (including high-voltage and EHV cables) for retail, institutional and EPC customers, plus a cable-EPC business.
KEI.revenueModel: Earns from selling wires and cables across retail and institutional channels and from cable-EPC projects. Volume growth, the retail/branded mix and metal costs drive revenue and margins.

CROMPTON.coreBusiness: A consumer-electricals company (Crompton Greaves Consumer Electricals) making fans, pumps, lighting and small home appliances, with leadership in fans and residential pumps, plus the Butterfly kitchen-appliance brand.
CROMPTON.revenueModel: Earns from selling branded consumer electricals through wide distribution. Category leadership (fans, pumps), premiumisation and the appliances push drive revenue.

VOLTAS.coreBusiness: A Tata Group cooling and engineering company (Voltas), the leading air-conditioner brand in India, with a room-AC and consumer-durables business (including the Voltas Beko JV) plus an electro-mechanical (HVAC) projects business.
VOLTAS.revenueModel: Earns from selling air conditioners and consumer durables (branded, seasonal, summer-heavy) and from executing electro-mechanical/HVAC projects. AC market leadership and project execution drive revenue.

BLUESTARCO.coreBusiness: An air-conditioning and commercial-refrigeration company (Blue Star) making room ACs and a large central-air-conditioning and cooling-products/projects business for commercial and industrial customers.
BLUESTARCO.revenueModel: Earns from selling room ACs (branded, seasonal) and from commercial AC/refrigeration products and projects. The mix of consumer cooling and B2B projects, plus market-share gains, drives revenue.

WHIRLPOOL.coreBusiness: The Indian arm of Whirlpool, making and selling home appliances — refrigerators, washing machines, air conditioners and microwaves — a major white-goods brand in India.
WHIRLPOOL.revenueModel: Earns from selling branded home appliances through wide distribution. Volumes, product mix and brand positioning drive revenue; demand is partly seasonal and discretionary.

DIXON.coreBusiness: India's largest electronics contract manufacturer (Dixon Technologies), making mobile phones, TVs, home appliances, lighting and other electronics for brands on an outsourced basis, benefiting from import-substitution and PLI incentives.
DIXON.revenueModel: Earns manufacturing margins (and government PLI incentives) on electronics built for brand customers — a high-volume, thin-margin contract-manufacturing model. Scale, customer additions and backward integration drive revenue.

KAYNES.coreBusiness: An end-to-end electronics manufacturing services (EMS) company (Kaynes Technology) building electronic systems for automotive, industrial, aerospace/defence, medical and other sectors, and expanding into semiconductor assembly/OSAT.
KAYNES.revenueModel: Earns manufacturing margins on electronics built for diverse industrial and OEM customers, with a higher-mix, higher-margin profile than consumer EMS. Order book, sector diversity and the semiconductor push drive revenue.

BSE.coreBusiness: One of India's two main stock exchanges (BSE Ltd), providing trading platforms for equities, derivatives, mutual funds and other instruments, plus listing, clearing and market-data services.
BSE.revenueModel: Earns transaction charges on trading (notably a fast-growing equity-derivatives business), listing fees, market-data sales and other services. Trading volumes — especially in derivatives — drive revenue.

MCX.coreBusiness: India's largest commodity-derivatives exchange (Multi Commodity Exchange), providing a platform for trading futures and options in commodities like gold, silver, crude oil and base metals.
MCX.revenueModel: Earns transaction fees on commodity-derivatives trading, plus membership and data fees. Trading volumes (tied to commodity-price volatility and participation) and the options franchise drive revenue.

ANGELONE.coreBusiness: A leading digital-first retail stockbroker (Angel One) offering app-based trading and investing in equities, derivatives, mutual funds and more, with a large and growing retail client base, plus newer lending and wealth products.
ANGELONE.revenueModel: Earns brokerage and transaction fees (heavily from derivatives trading), interest on client funding (margin), and distribution fees. Active-client growth and trading volumes drive revenue; regulation on derivatives is a key variable.

HDFCAMC.coreBusiness: One of India's largest mutual-fund managers (HDFC Asset Management), managing equity, debt and other funds for retail and institutional investors, backed by the HDFC brand and distribution.
HDFCAMC.revenueModel: Earns management fees calculated as a percentage of assets under management (AUM) — a scalable, annuity-like model. AUM growth, the equity-versus-debt mix (equity earns higher fees) and markets drive revenue.

NAM-INDIA.coreBusiness: A large mutual-fund manager (Nippon Life India Asset Management) running equity, debt, ETF and other funds for retail and institutional investors, with a notably strong ETF and retail franchise.
NAM-INDIA.revenueModel: Earns AUM-based management fees; AUM growth, the product mix (equity and ETFs) and market levels drive revenue — a scalable, annuity-like model.

UTIAMC.coreBusiness: One of India's oldest mutual-fund managers (UTI Asset Management), running equity, debt and other funds plus managing retirement and institutional mandates (including a large PMS/retirement-fund business).
UTIAMC.revenueModel: Earns AUM-based management fees across mutual funds and institutional/retirement mandates. AUM growth, product mix and market levels drive revenue.

NUVAMA.coreBusiness: A wealth-management and capital-markets firm (Nuvama Wealth Management, formerly Edelweiss Wealth) serving high-net-worth and affluent clients with wealth management, asset management, and institutional/investment-banking services.
NUVAMA.revenueModel: Earns fees and commissions from wealth management (advisory and distribution), asset management (AUM-based) and capital-markets/broking services. Client assets, transaction activity and HNI growth drive revenue.

NAUKRI.coreBusiness: India's leading internet-classifieds company (Info Edge), owner of the dominant job portal Naukri.com, plus 99acres (real estate), Jeevansathi (matrimony) and Shiksha (education) — and a major early investor in startups like Zomato and PolicyBazaar.
NAUKRI.revenueModel: Earns recurring subscription and listing fees from recruiters and advertisers across its portals (Naukri being the profit engine), plus value from its large startup-investment portfolio. Recruitment-market health drives the core business.

POLICYBZR.coreBusiness: India's largest online insurance and lending marketplace (PB Fintech), running Policybazaar (comparing and selling insurance policies) and Paisabazaar (comparing credit products), connecting consumers with insurers and lenders.
POLICYBZR.revenueModel: Earns commissions from insurers on policies sold and fees from lenders on credit products, plus advisory and platform services. Premium volumes, new-business growth and a shift toward profitability drive revenue.

PAYTM.coreBusiness: A digital-payments and financial-services company (One97 Communications, Paytm) offering consumer and merchant payments (QR, soundbox, wallet), plus distribution of loans, and merchant commerce/financial services.
PAYTM.revenueModel: Earns from payment-processing margins and device subscriptions (e.g. soundboxes), commissions on distributed loans and financial products, and merchant services. Payment volumes (GMV), merchant base and loan distribution drive revenue.

NYKAA.coreBusiness: A consumer-internet retailer (FSN E-Commerce, Nykaa) selling beauty and personal-care products online and through stores, plus a fashion vertical and its own private-label brands.
NYKAA.revenueModel: Earns retail margins on beauty and fashion sales (online and offline), boosted by higher-margin private labels, plus advertising/marketplace income. Beauty (the profitable core) and private-label growth drive revenue.

SWIGGY.coreBusiness: A consumer-internet company (Swiggy) running a major food-delivery platform and the Instamart quick-commerce (grocery) business, plus dining-out and logistics services.
SWIGGY.revenueModel: Earns commissions and delivery/platform fees from restaurants and customers on food delivery, retail margins and fees on quick-commerce grocery, plus advertising. Order volumes and the path to quick-commerce profitability drive revenue.

ETERNAL.coreBusiness: A consumer-internet company (Eternal, formerly Zomato) that is the parent of food-delivery platform Zomato, quick-commerce business Blinkit (now larger than food delivery), B2B food-supplies arm Hyperpure, and events platform District.
ETERNAL.revenueModel: Earns commissions and delivery/platform fees on food delivery, retail margins and fees on Blinkit's quick-commerce grocery, plus B2B supplies and events. Blinkit's growth and quick-commerce economics increasingly drive the business.

NAZARA.coreBusiness: A diversified gaming and interactive-entertainment company (Nazara Technologies) spanning mobile games, gamified learning, esports and ad-tech, built largely through acquisitions of gaming studios and platforms.
NAZARA.revenueModel: Earns from in-game purchases and subscriptions, esports and gamified-learning fees, and gaming ad-tech. A portfolio/acquisition-led model; user engagement and monetisation across its properties drive revenue.

BAJAJHLDNG.coreBusiness: An investment holding company of the Bajaj Group (Bajaj Holdings & Investment) whose value comes mainly from large stakes in Bajaj Auto and Bajaj Finserv, plus a portfolio of other investments. It is not an operating business in its own right.
BAJAJHLDNG.revenueModel: Earns dividend income from its holdings and gains on its investment portfolio. Its market value tracks the worth of its underlying stakes (chiefly Bajaj Auto and Bajaj Finserv), typically at a holding-company discount.

SUPREMEIND.coreBusiness: India's largest plastic-products company (Supreme Industries), making plastic piping systems (its biggest business), packaging products, industrial and moulded products, and consumer plastics for construction, agriculture and industry.
SUPREMEIND.revenueModel: Earns from selling plastic products — led by PVC/CPVC piping for plumbing, agriculture and infrastructure — through a wide distribution network. Volumes, value-added mix and polymer (crude-linked) input costs drive revenue and margins.

WAAREEENER.coreBusiness: India's largest maker of solar photovoltaic (PV) modules (Waaree Energies), with the country's biggest module capacity and a large export business, now vertically integrating backward into solar cells, wafers and ingots, plus inverters and storage.
WAAREEENER.revenueModel: Earns from selling solar modules to domestic developers and a large export market (notably the US), plus EPC and other solar products. Capacity expansion, backward integration (improving margins) and the global solar build-out drive revenue.

PREMIERENE.coreBusiness: An integrated solar-energy manufacturer (Premier Energies), one of India's largest makers of solar cells and modules, with a relatively strong position in solar-cell manufacturing alongside modules, serving domestic and export markets.
PREMIERENE.revenueModel: Earns from selling solar cells and modules to developers and other manufacturers, in India and as exports, plus EPC and O&M. Its solar-cell capacity (scarcer than module capacity in India) supports margins; capacity additions drive growth.

TMPV.coreBusiness: The passenger-vehicle arm of the Tata Group, created in the 2025 demerger of Tata Motors. It makes passenger cars and SUVs and electric vehicles in India and owns the global luxury carmaker Jaguar Land Rover. (It carries the original Tata Motors listing history; the ticker changed to TMPV in October 2025.)
TMPV.revenueModel: Earns from selling passenger and electric vehicles in India and luxury cars worldwide through Jaguar Land Rover, plus spare parts. JLR is by far the largest revenue contributor; the India PV/EV business and the JLR cycle are the key drivers.

TMCV.coreBusiness: India's largest commercial-vehicle maker (renamed Tata Motors Ltd), created as a standalone listed company in the 2025 demerger of Tata Motors. It makes trucks, buses and small commercial/goods vehicles across the light-to-heavy range, plus related services and an emerging CV electrification push.
TMCV.revenueModel: Earns from selling commercial vehicles — from small cargo carriers to medium and heavy trucks and buses — plus spare parts and services. Revenue is cyclical, tracking the freight, logistics, infrastructure and replacement-demand cycle; it holds the leading domestic CV market share.
